summary: The Fall of Saigon on April 30, 1975, was a pivotal moment in Vietnam's history when the North Communist Vietnam Party took control. It forced millions, including my mother and uncle, to flee the country by boat in search of safety and a better life. This event marked the beginning of a difficult journey for many Vietnamese refugees.

Vietnam is where my family’s story begins, especially during the turbulent times around 1975. After the Fall of Saigon, my mother and uncle had to leave Vietnam in 1981 to escape the hardships and political changes. Though they were separated from the rest of our family, Vietnam remains the place of our roots and the starting point of their courageous journey.

My mother and uncle were among the Vietnamese boat people who fled Vietnam by sea after the Fall of Saigon. Their journey was perilous, lasting about a week with scarce food and water, and the constant fear of pirates like the Thai pirates that many others encountered. Their survival and eventual rescue symbolize the resilience of all Vietnamese boat people during that era.

After escaping Vietnam by boat, my mother and uncle arrived at the Malaysian refugee camp called Pulau Bidong. They stayed there for about five and a half months, sharing the space with many other refugees while waiting for their next steps. Pulau Bidong was a crucial stop in their journey toward resettlement.

Following Pulau Bidong, my mother and uncle were transferred to another refugee camp in the Philippines called Bataan. They stayed there for another five and a half months while their paperwork was processed for entry into the United States. Bataan was an important transitional place during their refugee journey.

The Philippines played a significant role in my family’s refugee journey, as my mother and uncle stayed in the Bataan refugee camp there for several months. It was during their time in the Philippines that their paperwork was processed for acceptance into the United States. The Philippines was a key location in the path to their new life.

The United States became the new home for my mother and uncle after they were accepted as refugees in 1982. They arrived in Seattle, Washington, sponsored by a couple named Mr. and Mrs. Cotton. The U.S. offered them opportunities despite language barriers, and it’s because of their sacrifices here that I have been able to pursue my dreams, including my Taekwondo career.

Seattle, Washington, was the first city my mother and uncle arrived in when they came to the United States in 1982. It was the place where their new life began after years of hardship and waiting in refugee camps. Seattle was the gateway to the opportunities that allowed my family to rebuild and thrive.

Washington state was where my mother and uncle first settled in the United States, specifically in Seattle. It was here that they began adjusting to life in a new country despite language barriers and cultural challenges. Washington was the starting point for their American journey before they later moved to Minnesota.

After some time in Washington, my mother and uncle relocated to Minnesota because of the Vietnamese community there and friends they knew from back in Vietnam. Minnesota became our family’s new home where my mother continued to work hard to support us. It’s where I grew up and was able to pursue my passion for Taekwondo.

Taekwondo has been a passion of mine, and thanks to the sacrifices my mother made, I was able to pursue it seriously here in the United States. In the summer of 2014, I made it onto the U.S. National Taekwondo Team, which was a proud moment for me and my family. Taekwondo represents not only my personal achievement but also the opportunities afforded to me by my mother’s journey.

Making the United States National Taekwondo Team in 2014 was a milestone in my life. Wearing USA on my back at the World Taekwondo Championships was an honor that reflected both my dedication and the sacrifices my mother made to bring our family here. Being part of this team connected me to my country and gave me a platform to represent the U.S. internationally.

I competed at the World Taekwondo Championships in Mexico as a member of the U.S. National Team in 2014. Winning a bronze medal there was a proud moment for me and my family, symbolizing the opportunities I have in this country. This event was a culmination of years of hard work and the sacrifices made by my mother to give me a better life.

Mexico was the host country for the 2014 World Taekwondo Championships where I proudly represented the United States. Competing there and winning a bronze medal was a highlight of my Taekwondo career. Mexico holds a special place in my story as the site where I achieved an important milestone on the international stage.
